text:
The unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) industry has grown significantly over the past decade. What once seemed like a small recreational technology or personal hobby has now become an important tool in many commercial industries, including infrastructure inspection, emergency response, agriculture, security, and transportation. Because unmanned aircraft are now operating in more complex environments and closer to sensitive facilities, I support the FAA’s proposed rule to create flight restrictions near certain fixed-site facilities.

Manned aircraft are held to a high legal and safety standard, and it is reasonable that unmanned aircraft should also be subject to strong rules when their operation could affect public safety, property, national security, or critical infrastructure. This proposed rule would help create clearer boundaries for UAS operators and would make the industry more professional and accountable.

I believe these restrictions can help prevent careless or irresponsible drone use while still allowing responsible operators to continue using UAS technology in safe and productive ways. Clear rules also help legitimate commercial operators by giving the public and businesses more confidence in the industry. For these reasons, I am in favor of the proposed regulation.

I have both safety and security concerns with drones being allowed in rail yards. First they are a major distraction and could cause a serious injury if you get distracted while doing a safety sensitive job. Getting distracted while operating an RCO could lead you to not hit the right control. On top of this, non FCC compliant drones could interfere with RCO operations. How can railroad workers know if a drone is compliant without a clear look. Or how can they determine a compliant or non compliant drone if you allow one and not the other.

Next is railroad security. Since 9/11 we have been instructed to approach trespassers and report any suspicious activity. Now with drone technology, terrorists can now search for hazardous materials on rail cars and identify the most dangerous all from not being on the property of known to railroad security officials. They can see further into yards and remain totally anonymous. Just as drones have been used by Ukraine to attack Russia, terrorists can now carry an explosive device and place it next to hazardous materials all from a distance and possibly without interference from railroad officials.

Please Ban ALL drones from railroad property.

Re: Transmittal of Public Comment - Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in

Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33
91 FR 24650 (May 6, 2026)
Dear Ms. Ferritto and Docket Operations:
Please find enclosed my formal comment on the above-referenced Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
(NPRM), submitted within the public comment period closing July 6, 2026. I respectfully request that
this letter and the attached comment be entered into the public docket as a single submission.
I submit these comments in my individual capacity, but I do so against the background of nearly four
decades of operational experience that bears directly on the safety, security, and Public Safety
integration issues raised by proposed 14 CFR part 74. A brief summary of my relevant qualifications
follows:
• Licensed Private Investigator - Thirty-eight (38) years licensed and in good standing with the
South Carolina Law Enforcement Division (SLED).
• FAA Remote Pilot, Part 107 -Active Part 107 Remote Pilot Certificate; operator of a DJI
Matrice 30T, DJI Mavic 3T, and Chasing Ml Pro tethered surface platform.
Page 1 of 3
7

Cover Letter - Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
• Chief Pilot, Spartanburg County (SC) Emergency Management Services - Responsible for
unmanned aircraft operations supporting SAR, hazardous-materials response, and multi-agency
emergency incidents.
• Level Ill Thermographer (UAS) - Certified at the highest commercial level for infrared
thermography conducted from unmanned platforms, including life-safety SAR and
infrastructure-inspection applications.
• Certified Instructor - Search and Rescue; Fugitive Apprehension; ALIVE Active Shooter
response; NIST/ BPERP (Body-Worn/ Personal Equipment/ Response Protocols); and Private
Investigator pre-licensure and continuing education.
• Executive Board Member - National Council of Investigation and Security Services (NCISS), the
principal national trade association representing licensed private investigators and security
professionals before federal agencies and Congress.
• Legislative Chairman - South Carolina Association of Legal Investigators (SCALI).
• Monitoring Center Administrator - GPS and Home Incarceration Program monitoring center,
providing 24/7 location-tracking and compliance services to court systems and law-enforcement
agencies.
These qualifications inform every element of the enclosed comment. As a Chief Pilot for a county
Emergency Management Service, I have personal, on-scene experience with the time-sensitive nature of
SAR and hazmat operations and the operational impossibility of facility-by-facility notification during an
active incident. As a Level Ill thermographer and Part 107 commercial operator, I understand the
routine, lawful, low-altitude missions that the proposed rule risks foreclosing if implemented without
refinement. As a SLED-licensed investigator with 38 years of practice and a national-board obligation
through NCISS, I have a particular concern that the rule provide a clear credibility pathway for
accountable, vetted commercial operators rather than treating all unmanned-aircraft activity as
presumptively suspect. And as the administrator of a GPS monitoring center, I am keenly aware of the
cybersecurity, data-retention, and chain-of-custody implications of any new federally mandated
notification or tracking system - issues addressed directly in the enclosed comment.
The enclosed comment is offered constructively. It supports the FAA's implementation of section 2209
of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 and the framework of proposed part 74, while
identifying eight specific areas where targeted refinements would better balance the protection of
critical infrastructure against the statutory public right of transit under 49 U.S.C. § 40103 and the
legitimate operational needs of Public Safety and accountable commercial aviators.
I am available to expand on any point in the enclosed comment, to provide additional operational detail
from county Emergency Management or SAR experience, or to coordinate further input through NCISS
or SCALI as the rulemaking proceeds. Thank you for your consideration and for the FAA's continued
work on the safe integration of unmanned aircraft into the National Airspace System.

Re: Public Comment on Notice of Proposed Rulemaking - Designation-Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircr

I support the FAA’s proposed rule to create a process for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions near certain fixed-site facilities. Sensitive facilities mentioned in this NPRM do face real safety and security risks from unauthorized drone operations.

However, I recommend that the final rule emphasizes practicality for UAS operators. The restrictions should be easy to find in official flight planning sources intended for UAS operators. Sensitive facilities may require UAS services. Part 74 should prioritize allowing UAS operations requested by the facility to be conducted without undue delay.

I am a railroad conductor in Los Nietos Ca with 21yrs of rail service for Union Pacific RR. The use of drones by local managers over industries that unload chlorine gas and other hazardous chemicals is a major safety concern for us. We are asked to be vigilant and be the eyes and ears for the company. Being vigilant and focused on my work is very difficult as it is without the idea of drones flying overhead. We never know if it’s just a manager observing us or a potential threat. These chemicals would be disastrous if something were to happen to these tank cars. It is our responsibility to be on the lookout for any suspicious activity but that task becomes difficult to distinguish between management and actual threat because of these drones. The use of drones to monitor crew conduct is posing a real world threat to lives in and around these facilities. It only takes one conductor to mistake an actual terrorist act for a managers drone which could result in many unnecessary lives lost to tragedy. The use of drones can not continue if we are to be concerned about the safety of railroad infrastructure and civilian lives.

June 2, 2026
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue, S.E., Room W58-213
Washington, D.C. 20590-0001

Re: Comments on Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03 – Designation – Restrict
the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility (NPRM)
Dear Federal Aviation Administration Rulemaking Team:
The Jackson County Port Authority, operating as Port Pascagoula, appreciates the opportunity to
provide comments on the Federal Aviation Administration's Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
concerning the designation of unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (UAFRs) around eligible
fixed-site facilities.
As Mississippi's largest port and one of the nation's leading strategic seaports, Port Pascagoula
serves as a critical component of the United States maritime transportation system. The Port
handles military cargo, energy products, breakbulk commodities, forest products, project cargo,
and other essential cargoes that support national defense, economic stability, and supply chain
resilience. As a Maritime Transportation Security Act (MTSA) regulated facility, Port
Pascagoula maintains approved Facility Security Plans, complies with all applicable Maritime
Security (MARSEC) requirements, and works closely with the U.S. Coast Guard, Department of
Homeland Security, federal law enforcement agencies, and industry partners to protect critical
infrastructure.
Port Pascagoula strongly supports the FAA's efforts to finally implement Section 2209 of the
FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016. Establishing a formal process for critical
infrastructure operators to request drone flight restrictions is an important and long-overdue step

toward addressing the growing risks posed by unauthorized unmanned aircraft systems operating
near sensitive facilities.
The threat posed by drones continues to evolve. Ports face increasing concerns regarding
unauthorized surveillance, intelligence gathering, operational disruption, cyber vulnerabilities,
and potential physical attacks against critical infrastructure. Recent incidents across the maritime
sector have demonstrated that unmanned aircraft can be used to collect sensitive information,
monitor vessel operations, and access areas that are otherwise secured from public view.
Port Pascagoula respectfully offers the following recommendations regarding the proposed rule:
Streamline Eligibility for MTSA-Regulated Maritime Facilities
Port Pascagoula strongly encourages the FAA to establish an expedited approval process for
maritime facilities already regulated under MTSA and operating under approved Facility
Security Plans.
Maritime facilities already undergo rigorous federal oversight and maintain extensive security
measures that have been reviewed and approved by the U.S. Coast Guard. Requiring ports to
recreate or duplicate security documentation for UAFR applications would create unnecessary
administrative burdens while providing little additional security value.
Accordingly, Port Pascagoula recommends that:
• MTSA-regulated facilities with approved Facility Security Plans automatically qualify to
apply for UAFRs;
• Existing Coast Guard security documentation be accepted as supporting evidence
whenever practical;
• Strategic seaports and nationally significant maritime infrastructure receive priority
review consideration.
Recognize Port Authorities as Eligible Governing Entities
Public port authorities are uniquely positioned to coordinate security across complex, multi-
tenant environments that include cargo terminals, energy facilities, rail infrastructure, industrial
operations, and maritime transportation facilities.

Port Pascagoula's jurisdiction includes multiple facilities and tenants operating under a unified
security framework. Requiring individual operators to seek separate flight restrictions could
create inconsistencies and administrative inefficiencies.
Port Pascagoula recommends that the FAA:
• Explicitly recognize port authorities as eligible applicants capable of submitting UAFR
requests on behalf of multiple facilities;
• Allow port authorities to coordinate applications covering public port property and
associated infrastructure;
• Clarify that individual tenant participation should not be required when a port authority is
acting within its statutory authority to protect critical infrastructure.
Allow Operational Flexibility Beyond Strict Property Boundaries
Ports operate in highly complex environments where security concerns often extend beyond
surveyed property lines.
Critical maritime infrastructure frequently includes waterways, navigation channels, rail
corridors, utility easements, roadways, and interconnected facilities that function as a single
operational system. Limiting UAFRs strictly to property boundaries may leave vulnerabilities
that could be exploited by malicious actors.
Port Pascagoula recommends that the FAA:
• Permit reasonable boundary extensions when justified by security requirements;
• Allow interconnected maritime and industrial facilities to be treated as a unified
operational area;
• Consider restrictions that account for critical waterways and navigation corridors adjacent
to secure facilities.
Improve Processing Capacity and Interagency Coordination
The FAA has acknowledged that a significant volume of applications may be received once the
rule is implemented.
To ensure timely and effective processing, Port Pascagoula recommends that the FAA:

• Expand coordination with the U.S. Coast Guard, Department of Homeland Security, and
other Sector Risk Management Agencies;
• Increase staffing and technical review capacity;
• Prioritize applications from facilities supporting national defense, energy security, and
critical supply chains;
• Develop efficien

Drones around the railroads in my view is a very bad idea as it creates safety concerns with drones being used as weapons. If bad actors know drones are used around railroads then they can weaponize them and create chaos and disarray with rail operations. Additionally this can compromise the health and safety of the workers on or around the railroad let alone the communities the railroads travel through. The railroad management if concerned about the workers productivity should drive to location or utilize the cameras they already have available to them or install closed circuit video cameras throughout the rail yards to eliminate their desire for drones.

I work for BNSF railway. And my job is already Dangerous and stressful. But when I see a drone flying.over my head in a rapid movement. My eyes direct to the fast moving object that just flew over my head in my line of site. When im riding on a train that exceeds 2 miles n  they want my full attention to make sure I do t kill someone. Its suddenly OK for Managment to start.fying a Drone over my head to watch me work. It is super unsafe cause with he theft thats going on the rail with Gang member.  How do I k ow if its Managment or a gang that watching g me work to come rob me. Super unsafe.

I have only been working for the railroad (CSX) as a conductor for the past two years. Over those two years I've watched managers put the safety of us employees and area locals in jeopardy with the use and abuse of aerial drones. Fact: droning us during operations does absolutely nothing to improve safety. Under FRA regulations must not engage in any unrelated tasks while performing shove movements. It is imperative that one stays focused for everyone's safety while controlling these movements. Now imagine trying to maintain focus, especially as a more inexperienced employee and even more so at night when out of no where someone is doing a fly by right in your field of vision and then hovering over your head with all of the flashing lights. At first you don't know what it is, then once you do realize that it's a manager breathing down your neck trying to find any little mistake that he can write you up on to try and take your job, thats when the nerves really kick in and at that point you probably forgot that you were in the middle of making a rearward movement with a long line of mixed explosive and poisonous inhalation cars.

I am a conductor for a freight railroad with over 5 years of experience. The drones management and even railfans use create a distraction in an already dangerous transportation area. They fly them overhead to get video footage for operations practices testing and in the case of railfans, video footage for their own personal enjoyment. They are loud and if you aren't expecting it can get reactions out of you or cause you to turn your head. With 143 ton rolling stock moving around being distracted for just a few seconds can cost you a limb or your life. A single incident, even if not causing bodily harm can cost the company tens of thousands of dollars which we are then disciplined for.

I strongly urge the FAA to adopt facility‑bound perimeter
The rule does create UAS‑only airspace discrimination if its 400' and lower, however if the airspace is restricted to anything below 600' it would block all aircraft from flying over these facilities.

If a facility is sensitive enough to warrant a federally enforced no‑fly zone, that restriction should apply consistently across all aircraft categories, not solely unmanned systems.

I believe that Railroads should NOT be able to fly drones near any yard, or mainline track, and any connection there of, due to the safety sensitive jobs of conductor and engineer. Drones can be distracting and cause accidents, not to mention giving the ability to management to fine any little infraction to dismiss or terminate you as a rail employee. Also, those that use drones are NOT FAA licensed, they do NOT follow FAA protocol when using drones, especially near airports and other facilities that are transporation safety sensitive.

I have been trained for 22 years that, "If you see something, Say something." In switching and local freight train operations, Managers are drone testing crews quite frequently. When the crews call them out on the radio and report it, they dismiss our concerns and sometimes ridicule us for pointing them out. In time, that discouragement and contestant drone presence will desensitize us to them. Hear is a self evident formula that may help illustrate the severity of this situation.

TIH/PIH and other hazardous commodity + UFO drone fly over + Managers disregarding reports + time = desensitized ground crews and a week point in our national security for terrorist attacks!

Air ports do not allow Drone flying activity. Railroads, Yards and rail Industries have much more critically sensitive hazardous equipment than airports. It seems logical and safer to restrict the airspace around all rail activities. A terrorist could easily cause major casualties with a drone. Please Keep our Rail air space clear and safe and protect our communities around them.

Conductor with 29yrs of service in transportation.
Drones are unsafe. If MTO want to watch and teach us come out in the field. The training is getting less and less we should not have to be looking up to see if we might be being watched. Come walk with me and watch and explain what I am doing right and wrong. I have been reprimanded for using the technology that they give us just told I did not use it as they believe it is to be used.

As a railroad conductor with 4 years of experience working in active rail operations, I have serious concerns about increasing drone activity in rail yards and around railroad infrastructure. My job requires constant awareness of moving equipment, radio communications, track conditions, and the safety of employees working on the ground. Adding drones into an already complex environment creates another distraction and potential hazard that railroad workers must account for.

Rail yards handle hazardous materials, high-value cargo, and critical infrastructure every day. From a conductor’s perspective, seeing drones operating overhead can create uncertainty about their purpose and whether they are authorized. This raises legitimate security concerns, especially in locations where hazardous chemicals are stored or transported.

The people who work in these environments every day understand the operational and safety challenges better than anyone. Before drone operations become commonplace in rail yards, the FAA should seek input from the conductors, engineers, and other frontline employees who will be directly affected by these decisions.

Rail safety depends on minimizing unnecessary risks, maintaining situational awareness, and protecting critical infrastructure. Any expansion of drone activity in rail operations should be approached with extreme caution and with the input of the railroad workers responsible for keeping these environments safe.

As a 20 year TY&E employee and a 20 year safety record, it has been widely known operations with and around any crew need to be discussed. It is also known that TY&E employees can be observed for operations testing and rules compliance per policy and safety standards over each craft. Will the carrier advise a crew of any findings, fault or no fault, from an Arial view or any other device or method used to as an observation point?

Will the carrier(s) provide prior knowledge to a crew member of any craft that has a diagnosis of PTSD that are from combat conditions that may create a reoccurance of Fog of War, that may raise anxiety, stress and break down of confidence when not knowing they are being observed with an aerial device?

Secondly, if any craft crew member is to report to his/her immediate supervisor that they believe an aerial devise is observing their operations of movement, will the carrier disclose in writing to any crew member that an aerial device is conducting operating testing? And will the findings, negative or favorable be relayed in operations testing review for the employee(s)?

Someone who has served under high impact, combat zones during multiple air assaults from Iraqi war from 2003 to 2006, this knowledge of being observed from an aerial devise has me on edge. If its unsafe for 1, its unsafe for all. That is to include non-employeed workers off the carriers property.

As someone who works in a rail yard at night, I’ve experienced firsthand how dangerous irresponsible drone operations can be. We had a drone hovering directly over our work area with a bright light shining down on us. In an environment where visibility is already limited and employees are focused on moving equipment safely, that kind of distraction creates an unnecessary safety risk. Rail yards are complex, hazardous workplaces, and operators need to be able to concentrate on their surroundings without interference from drones overhead. The FAA’s proposed rule recognizes a real concern that railroad workers have been raising for years.

I am a conductor for NS for 7 years. Drones create unnecessary distractions for employees of all types in and around railyards, and main tracks. Communication and attention is vital at all times and even just a short span of distraction can result in catastrophic accidents, including death to oneself or others.

Foremost, it is a hazard to national security. Railroads are the backbone of the U.S. economy and allowing usage of them would make it impossible to distinguish terrorist threats vs routine surveillance.

Secondly, I have witnessed first hand. While switching cars on the main, I've had a residential drone get withing just a few feet of myself while performing duties. It resulted in contacting local and railroad police, but this is a real life experience that has happened, and would be encouraged to happen more frequently if this was allowed.

As a Frontline worker, I urge you to consider this proposal and vote on the side of safety. Not just for the railroad workers, but for the community surrounding rail traffic.

As a veteran rail professional with 23 years of experience serving as both a conductor and a locomotive engineer, I have a deep understanding of operational safety and have trained numerous new hire employees on compliance and situational awareness. A core tenet of railroad safety is maintaining strict focus on the task at hand. The introduction of drone operations in the vicinity of active rail lines introduces a significant, unnecessary distraction that compromises crew focus. This distraction heightens the risk of critical operational errors, potentially leading to derailments, equipment damage, injuries, or fatalities. Furthermore, integrating this technology introduces cybersecurity vulnerabilities, specifically regarding the risk of unauthorized drone hacking, which presents an additional threat to rail security and operational integrity.

Railroading experience of 3 years engineer and conductor certified. The use of drones in railroads for safety audits and crew overseeing is a hazard in it self, from bringing distractions drawing attention from important railroad related movements and along with unkowning if a drone is a safety team, personal, or someone will ill intent. This could cause us railroad workers to ignore them and would never know if it’s a threat or not. Also noting the use of drones could effect local air traffic noting most rail yards for my company are located near military/ airports

Your craft and years of railroad experience.
That active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead.
That frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety.
That drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns.
That railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments.
That normalizing drones flying over thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests.
Tell them your truth about drones in rail yards anyway you see fit.

My name is Scott Newton, and I have 23 years of seniority with Union pacific Railroad. Ground service employees have a plethora of safety responsibilities we must adhere to in addition to hitting (sometimes) unrealistic job performance goals set by managers with less manpower. Having drones fly overhead is just one additional distraction we do not need to safely perform our job. I see employees looking up for drones more than ever in my career. Taking their eyes off of pathways or their job at-hand is going to get someone hurt, or worse.

In addition, when I see a drone overhead in a rail yard, I have no idea if it belongs to a manager, a civilian or some type of terroristic organization. Railyards are, by far, the least secure transportation hub in the United States. Once foreign or domestic terrorists' figure that out, not only employees but citizens will be in grave danger.

The time to act is now and put a hard restriction on the use of drones over transportation hubs such as railyards.

Personally the flying of drones in rail yards I really unsafe. It’s a distraction that can cause us, trainmen, to look away from the task at hand. That would potentially lead to injury, property damage, and/or fatality. Even the sound of it can cause us not to hear a moving car that’s been kicked in our direction. It should not be any distractions in the field. It’s so many factors out there that we have to continually have our head on a swivel.

I operate a remote control locomotive for the railroad. This is a job that requires that I do the job of what used to be the job of 3 different employees by myself. The drones that fly and buzz around me have been a distraction of exponential magnitude that is completely unneeded and unnecessary when moving hazards chemicals. We have had at least one drone crash in our yard, which could have easily caused an accident. I have had my attention distracted from the buzzing of a drone over my head. We have enough to focus on when handling hazmat tanks cars via remote control or while working with and engineer to have our attention diverted by a drone’s humming and buzzing over our head while working, wondering whether the drones will crash or distract our coworkers and cause an accident.

I was working a road switcher. While putting my last cut of cars in a yard where there is a platform (Metrolink) I noticed a drone hovering as I was stepping down to the ROW (right of way, rocks next to the tracks). My heel from my boot caught the edge of the platform and I fell onto the rail. All because I was looking up while stepping down. If my cut of cars was longer or my engineer would have been faster, I would of been killed. I was out of work for a month due to my femur bone and my hip being in pain from the fall. Bottom line... I had my work keys hooked to my radio harness, my radio in my left hand, my lantern in my right hand, my list in between my fingers, my clear lensed PPE which has no Peripheral vision issued by the company. Weather was cold so I was dressed warm which also limited my quick movements and reaction while on the rail. We are living in an era where drones are the future and air space is limitless as opposed to ground space ie: private property signs, guard rails, do not trespass etc. I understand the reason for arial audits, only if it pertains to the actual SAFETY of our railroaders, meaning the elements of nature when our workers are in the middle of the wilderness working on a train faced with the wild life, or working on a train in the inner cities where looters, angry homeless people, drug addicts, gang bangers are literally out numbering our single conductors walking the train in the night hours with hands already holding a radio and lantern and no form of defense. My conclusion, to the operators of these railroad drones; we are the front lines risking our lives. How is trying to manage safety from the sky going to save lives? What is it that you want? to catch a fatality from the sky on your hard drive? If safety is a concern... lace up your steel toe stacy adams and come out with us.. and get in the mix. We laugh, we smile, we eat, we breathe, we facetime our kids when we can, we also sweat, we bleed, we cry, and sometimes we pass away while on duty. We take our craft seriously. Please, one less distraction whether it be on ground or in the air..can assist in our brothers and sister coming home to families and friends.

Respectfully,

just a railroader.

Hello my name is Michael. I have worked for a class one railroad for 13 years. At the beginning of my currier at the railroad a group of business men from china came to one of our yards and brought out the drones to test them. They told us how they were going to help us do our work. Inspect tracks, help protect shoving moments and help in safety related situations. I just smiled and said we will see. The man asked me why I said that. I told him because the only reason my company wants drones is going to be to record employees working and try told fire them for rule violations. He insisted that was the case and I told him to come back in ten years.  Fast forward 13 years and I have yet to use a drone to protect a shove or inspect a rail. Any time I have seen a drone is when it flies over my head recording me work as my manager watches every move I make to make sure I don’t violate one of the tens of thousands of rules I am required to follow. They are extremely distracting for us employees. We are working in a very dangerous environment where we are not aloud to use any electronic devices because we can not be distracted during our work but my company thinks it is appropriate to have a drone flying 10 feet over my head circling me while I work and that is not a distraction. The noise they make and flashing lights I see out of the corner of my eye is also a major distraction that could cause me to loose focus and injure myself. Also, we are not told we are being watched by company drones. When we ask if it is a manager flying a drone above is the response we get is we do not own the sky keep working. In the current climate of war that we live in where modern warfare is fought by drones and our railyards are safety sensitive targets it only makes sense not to allow drone activity fly above employees causing them to fear for there safety. If you don’t understand what I am saying let me fly a drone ten feet over your head the next time you are out front washing your car or doing yard work on at your house and tell me how that feels. Thank your for your time and listening to my issues with the drone program the railroads use to bully and intimidate employees.

So I will keep it simple. I’m a freight engineer I was a conductor for 10 years now an engineer for 3 years. I understand testing is a requirement and has to be done. With that being said with all of the differand situations currently going on in our world using drones for the purpose of monitoring or testing is nothing but a distraction. We are told to look out for anything out of the ordinary for possible terrorism or trespassing I personally can’t tell the difference between someone testing, a citizen train watching, or possibly someone trying to deliver an IED from the air to a hazardous of poison inhalation load which would be catastrophic to the workers and the public. We always say see something say something well I’m saying something. We already have multiple distractions throughout our day and already are in extremely safety sensitive positions and adding another one in the mix is totally not worth it just for rules testing they have be able to test fine for years without them not sure why they need them now. If drones are needed are needed for security reasons then they should only be operated by the railroads law enforcement agents only for problem areas and the should be trained at an FAA licensed facility and licensed for each type of drone that will be used and crews notified when they will be in use in the area to limit wasting valuable resources investigating reports of drones in the area I believe railroad infrastructure such as railyards and main lines should be restricted airspace, just as airports, unless operated by licensed and qualified enforcement agencies for our security. We are in a time that security is a must. We as employees should be able to work, not having to worry if someone is there trying to cause a mass casualty event and if someone is there and restricted airspace, they now will be tracked and they can be spoken to. Thank you for your time and thank you for allowing comments.

RE: Public Comment on Proposed Rules for Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) Operations Over Critical Infrastructure and Fixed Site Facilities (Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016)

As the Director of Flight Services for Banner Health, with over 30 years of experience in critical care and emergency medicine, I am writing to submit formal public comments regarding the implementation of Section 2209. Helicopter Emergency Medical Services (HEMS) and fixed-wing air ambulance operations represent a unique, highly vulnerable segment of low-altitude aviation. While we strongly support the technological advancement, economic potential, and standard commercial deployment of the Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) industry, the safety of manned emergency medical aircraft must remain paramount.
The threat environment surrounding low-altitude airspace has evolved dramatically, as seen in both well-documented domestic civilian near-misses and shifting global military security contexts. To preserve the integrity of life-saving medical flights and national security the FAA’s final framework for Section 2209 must mandate absolute deference to manned emergency aircraft and tightly regulate UAS operations over and around critical health infrastructure.
The Problem: Immediate Low-Altitude Conflict
The critical intersection between HEMS operations and unregulated or non-compliant UAS operations is not a theoretical hazard; it is an active, escalating operational crisis.
Earlier today, on June 2, 2026, our flight program experienced a severe near-midair collision that underscores the exact regulatory gaps Section 2209 must address. One of our HEMS aircraft was executing a departure from a rural hospital landing zone in Grand County, Colorado. During the critical phase, a civilian Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) significantly exceeded its altitude limitations, operating directly within the departure corridor. The UAV came within 100 feet of our aircraft.
This incident highlights two fatal flaws in the current low-altitude ecosystem:
1 Inability to Deconflict in Real Time: Manned aircraft crews currently have zero capability to communicate directly with unmanned vehicle operators during an in-flight emergency. When a HEMS crew identifies a visual conflict at 500 feet Above Ground Level (AGL), there is no common radio frequency, digital handshake, or immediate traffic coordination mechanism to resolve the spatial conflict.
2 The Vulnerability of Uncontrolled Landing Zones: Hospital helipads, temporary scene calls (such as highway accidents), and rural landing zones are highly dynamic environments. UAS hobbyists and non-urgent commercial operators routinely operate nearby without awareness of incoming, time-critical medical flights.
The Imperative for Strict Regulation under Section 2209
The FAA must recognize fixed-site healthcare facilities, specifically all hospitals with designated heliports and trauma centers as critical infrastructure under Section 2209. Non-urgent commercial operations and hobbyist flights must be prohibited within these defined airspaces unless specifically authorized and integrated into an automated, real-time deconfliction network.
Our position is anchored on three core principles:
Absolute Right-of-Way and Deference: The FAA must codify an unyielding hierarchy of airspace priority. Manned aircraft, particularly emergency medical and public safety assets, must always be granted automatic deference over unmanned hobbyists and non-urgent commercial activities. A hobbyist photograph or a routine commercial package delivery must never compete with a patient requiring medical transport.
Mandatory, Interoperable Digital Deconfliction: Future rules must move beyond passive "Remote ID" tracking and enforce active, latency-free geofencing around critical infrastructure. If a HEMS asset activates a flight plan or transmits an emergency transponder code, nearby UAS networks should automatically receive a command to yield, descend, or land.
Balanced Industry Integration: We recognize that the UAS industry provides massive societal value, including the future potential for rapid blood, organ, and medical supply delivery. However, the commercial scaling of this industry cannot outpace the foundational safety architectures required to protect human lives in the sky and on the ground.
Conclusion
The near-miss in Grand County today could have resulted in a catastrophic loss of life, the destruction of a vital multi-million dollar air medical asset, and the denial of emergency care to a critical patient. As the FAA finalizes the rules for Section 2209, I urge the rulemaking committee to enact strict, enforceable boundaries around fixed-site healthcare facilities and mandate absolute right-of-way protocols for manned emergency aircraft.
Thank you for your time, consideration, and continued commitment to aviation safety.

Sincerely,
Joe Darmofal

I am a conductor at a class one railroad. I have worked as a conductor for 8 years now and I have been here through a lot of technological implementation, good and bad. I am writing this comment today concerning drone usage by railroad carriers for operations testing and employee surveillance.

I respectfully oppose the FAA's proposed Part 74 Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR) rule.

While protecting critical infrastructure, public safety, and national security are legitimate objectives, this proposal risks creating a nationwide patchwork of permanent drone exclusion zones that will undermine the FAA's parallel obligation to integrate unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) into the National Airspace System (NAS).

The proposal authorizes restrictions for a broad range of facilities, including energy infrastructure, chemical facilities, communications networks, data centers, manufacturing sites, defense contractors, commercial facilities, dams, and prisons. While each category may appear reasonable individually, the FAA fails to adequately analyze the cumulative effect of potentially thousands of permanent or long-term restrictions across the country.

Congress directed the FAA to balance security concerns with airspace efficiency and the public right of transit. This proposal places too much emphasis on speculative risks and not enough on preserving access to public airspace.

A central concern is that the rule effectively allows private entities to seek government-enforced restrictions over airspace above their property. Property ownership does not confer ownership of navigable airspace. Public airspace should remain public unless restrictions are supported by clear, documented, and recurring threats. Many facilities may prefer that drones not operate nearby, but preference alone should not justify limiting access to a public resource.

The proposal repeatedly focuses on what could happen if a drone were misused. However, regulatory restrictions should be based on demonstrated risks rather than theoretical possibilities. Nearly every facility can identify a hypothetical drone threat. The FAA should require evidence of documented incidents, credible intelligence, or repeated unlawful activity before approving a UAFR.

The FAA also overlooks the fact that existing laws already prohibit reckless drone operations, contraband delivery, criminal surveillance, sabotage, interference with emergency response, and other dangerous conduct. Additional restrictions will primarily burden compliant operators while doing little to deter individuals already willing to violate federal law.

The economic impact of this proposal is also underestimated. Commercial drones support agriculture, infrastructure inspection, surveying, construction, environmental monitoring, journalism, and emergency response. Expanding restricted airspace increases compliance costs, flight planning complexity, operational inefficiencies, and barriers to innovation. These burdens will fall most heavily on small businesses, independent operators, and startups rather than large corporations with dedicated compliance staff.

The proposal's reliance on Remote ID monitoring also raises privacy concerns. Facilities seeking UAFRs would be required to receive Remote ID broadcasts, and the FAA is considering data retention requirements. Remote ID was intended to support accountability and safety, not to create a nationwide network of private entities collecting and storing information about lawful drone operators. Any retention requirement should be rejected absent a compelling and demonstrated need.

The strongest justification presented in the proposal concerns correctional facilities, where documented incidents involving contraband delivery by drones are well established. However, evidence supporting restrictions for prisons should not automatically justify restrictions across every sector listed in the rule. Restrictions should be narrowly tailored to demonstrated threats.

The FAA should revise this proposal by requiring documented evidence of need, prioritizing temporary restrictions over permanent ones, limiting restricted areas to the smallest practical footprint, requiring periodic review and automatic expiration, and strengthening transparency requirements.

The National Airspace System is a public resource. Restrictions on access should be narrowly tailored, evidence-based, and demonstrably necessary. As written, the proposed rule is overly broad and risks undermining innovation, economic growth, and the successful integration of UAS into American airspace. I urge the FAA to substantially narrow the proposal before adoption.

commenter: Comment from Montana Department of Transportation
text:
Montana Department of Transportation
Response to FAA Proposed Rule: Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
June 3, 2026
Docket Operations
Federal Aviation Administration
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590
Subject: Comments on FAA NPRM, Restricting UAS Operations
Near Fixed-Site Facilities
The Montana Department of Transportation (MDT) appreciates the opportunity to
comment on the Federal Aviation Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking titled
‘Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility,’
published in the Federal Register on May 6, 2026. MDT supports efforts that balance
national security, public safety, and the safe integration of unmanned aircraft systems
(UAS) into the national airspace system.
MDT operates a robust and growing UAS program that supports critical transportation
missions statewide. These include roadway inspections, bridge and structure inspection
support, stockpile measurement, rockslide monitoring along high-risk corridors,
photogrammetric mapping, and incident response. MDT is also evaluating future
automated and remote UAS capabilities to support rapid monitoring rockslides, flooding
hazards, and other emergent transportation issues across Montana’s challenging
operating environment.
While MDT recognizes and supports the FAA’s objective of improving safety around fixed-
site facilities, several aspects of the proposed rule raise concerns regarding potential
impacts on MDT’s ability to conduct essential UAS operations. In particular, the location,
size, and administration of Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) could
unintentionally affect transportation corridors and bridges, tunnels, maintenance areas,
and hazard monitoring locations that fall within or near UAFR boundaries. MDT is
concerned that UAFR boundaries associated with eligible fixed-site facilities may overlap
with or restrict access to operational areas where recurring UAS missions occur,
particularly in mountainous regions prone to avalanches, as well as around bridges,
tunnels, or maintenance facilities. Restrictions in these areas could hinder MDT’s ability
to perform timely hazard assessments, infrastructure inspections, and automated
monitoring flights that protect both agency personnel and the traveling public.

The proposed rule may also create administrative and operational challenges. MDT
manages numerous dynamic and geographically dispersed hazard areas that do not align
cleanly with fixed-site definitions. Requiring individual reviews or UAFR-related approvals
for each site could place significant burdens on both MDT and the FAA, potentially slowing
emergency responses and degrading situational awareness during critical events.
MDT respectfully recommends that the FAA revise the proposed rule to incorporate more
flexible provisions for state transportation agencies and emergency responders. This
should include clarifying operational allowances for infrastructure inspection, public safety,
emergency response, disaster assessment, and transportation hazard monitoring within
or near designated UAFRs, including how those allowances apply to Part 107 operations,
public aircraft operations, and future authorized BVLOS or automated UAS operations.
MDT further recommends that the FAA consider a streamlined process for agencies with
broad statewide operational responsibilities, reducing duplicative administrative
requirements and enabling consistent, efficient UAS mission planning across diverse
operating environments.
MDT also recommends that UAFR locations, boundaries, requirements, points of contact,
and status information be made available in a machine-readable format. This would allow
public agencies to integrate UAFR information into GIS systems, mission planning tools,
forms, dashboards, and other operational workflows.
In conclusion, MDT supports the FAA’s intention to enhance safety and security but urges
the agency to ensure that any final rule incorporates practical allowances for
transportation-related UAS operations. MDT remains committed to collaborating with the
FAA to strike a balance between safeguarding sensitive facilities and ensuring that
essential public-service UAS operations can continue without unnecessary restrictions or
delays.
Sincerely,
Montana Department of Transportation
Unmanned Aircraft Systems Program

Not only airspace infringments but fluing over private properties at very low altitudes.  I have observed several instances that apperances that rules were broken.  I think that there are places drones could be more useful to railroads vs flying in communities where public safety is at risk

To Whom It May Concern,

I am writing to raise the serious safety concern regarding the use of unmanned aircraft systems, commonly referred to as drones, by railroad management to monitor employees and initiate disciplinary referrals against train and engine service employees working in active rail yards.

Railroad operations, whether conducted within a yard or over the line of road, are inherently safety-sensitive. Trainmen are required to maintain constant situational awareness while working around moving equipment, switches, shove movements, close clearances, radio communications, and other employees on the ground. Any unnecessary distraction introduced into that environment creates an unacceptable risk.

The use of drones flying above or near employees actively engaged in switching operations is not merely intrusive; it is dangerous. These aircraft create visual and auditory distractions in an environment where a momentary loss of focus can result in serious injury or death. Employees naturally look toward, react to, or become aware of drones operating overhead, and that distraction can interfere with their ability to safely perform duties that already demand undivided attention.

This practice is especially needless because railroads already maintain extensive fixed-camera surveillance throughout rail yard facilities. The use of drones in addition to existing camera systems is absolutely unnecessary for legitimate operational oversight and is solely designed to generate disciplinary referrals. Using aerial surveillance in this manner introduces a new hazard into an already hazardous workplace without any corresponding safety benefit.

In short, the deployment of drones over active rail operations for employee surveillance and discipline is an unnecessary and unsafe practice. It creates foreseeable distractions that can contribute to operational errors with potentially fatal consequences. I respectfully request that the FAA deny, through regulation, this practice as it diverts employee attention in a setting where even a brief distraction can result in catastrophic injury or death.

Drones are not the answer. They are very disruptive! All they are doing is having conductors pay more attention to the drones then concentrating on the task at hand. They aren’t a safe way to conduct testing. In my opinion it’s just laziness on the company’s behalf. The management needs to talk to crews face to face not watch them with a drone!
If the thing crashes what damage will it cause? I’m not a fan of them.

Drones are cool and useful but should only be used when it's appropriate. Hovering over rail workers while they are switching in a yard is not one of those places. Instead of the worker looking down watching their footing, they are now looking up because of buzzing they hear. We as employees don't know who is behind the drone as we have not been trained on how to distinguish an approved use - it could be anyone!

I've heard of my coworkers who have been sitting on a locomotive and a drone just comes flying up to the window to snoop on them. How many times can a manager do that before it hits the employee in the face? The carrier's will say that never happens, but the only way to make that statement true, is to not allow them in the first place.

Conductor for Class 1 RR

Executive Summary of Attached Public Comment

Please see the attached document for my full, comprehensive analysis of the Section 2209 NPRM. Below is an executive summary of the critical logical, legal, and operational flaws identified in the proposed rule:

1. The "Slippery Slope" of Connectivity: Defining "critical facilities" by their systemic connections (e.g., sewers feeding a plant, or distant runoff pipes) creates an absurd chain logic. Because much of our infrastructure is subterranean, broad interpretations would allow entire municipalities to lock down public skies. Recommendation: Restrictions must be strictly limited to the immediate, physical footprint of the core asset.

2. Data Security Inconsistency: Restricting low-altitude sUAS out of espionage or privacy fears is redundant. High-powered ground-based cameras, manned Part 91 aircraft, and commercial satellite imagery already legally collect superior data. Restricting drones protects nothing; it only penalizes a single technology sector.

3. The Remote ID Paradox: Blanket geographic bans undermine the FAA's own Remote ID (RID) framework. If a drone transparently broadcasts its identity and pilot location, it poses no covert threat. Because bad actors will simply disable RID, blanket airspace bans solely punish and ground compliant pilots.

4. Erosion of Federal Preemption: Handing veto power over public airspace to private railroad executives, corporate entities, and local utility boards effectively outsources federal sovereignty. It threatens to turn the unified National Airspace System (NAS) into a fragmented patchwork of corporate fiefdoms.

5. Economic and Maintenance Self-Sabotage: Infrastructure operators are the primary beneficiaries of drone inspections. Overreaching restrictions will trap third-party environmental teams, emergency services, and facility contractors in a bureaucratic logjam waiting for FAA waivers to conduct safety-essential maintenance.

6. The "Neighborhood Watch" Blind Spot: Compliant drone pilots act as a decentralized layer of public oversight. They are often the first to spot and report brush fires, chemical leaks, structural failures, or trespassers. Total bans create a security vacuum by clearing the sky of the "good guys."

7. The Solution (Micro-Airspaces & Passive Disruption): Infrastructure is deeply integrated into the public spaces it serves. Rather than granting sweeping, multi-mile exclusion zones that paralyze local economies, the FAA should mandate tight micro-airspaces combined with localized, automated, and passive perimeter-disruption systems that target threats without grounding the public.

Conclusion:
The FAA must protect the unity of the NAS, honor the utility of Remote ID, and require an extraordinary burden of proof from facility managers before privatizing public skies.

Full arguments, examples, and detailed recommendations are provided in the attached PDF.

Eric Nicolaysen
Owner / HighRes LLC

Dear Ms. Ferritto and Docket Operations:
Please find enclosed my formal comment on the above-referenced Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
(NPRM), submitted within the public comment period closing July 6, 2026. I respectfully request that
this letter and the attached comment be entered into the public docket as a single submission.
I submit these comments in my individual capacity, but I do so against the background of nearly four
decades of operational experience that bears directly on the safety, security, and Public Safety
integration issues raised by proposed 14 CFR part 74. A brief summary of my relevant qualifications
follows:
• Licensed Private Investigator - Thirty-eight (38) years licensed and in good standing with the
South Carolina Law Enforcement Division (SLED).
• FAA Remote Pilot, Part 107 - Active Part 107 Remote Pilot Certificate; operator of a OJI
Matrice 30T, OJI Mavic 3T, and Chasing Ml Pro tethered surface platform.

Cover Letter - Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
• Chief Pilot, Spartanburg County (SC) Emergency Management Services - Responsible for
unmanned aircraft operations supporting SAR, hazardous-materials response, and multi-agency
emergency incidents.
• Level Ill Thermographer (UAS) - Certified at the highest commercial level for infrared
thermography conducted from unmanned platforms, including life-safety SAR and
infrastructure-inspection applications.
• Certified Instructor - Search and Rescue; Fugitive Apprehension; ALIVE Active Shooter
response; NIST/ BPERP (Body-Worn/ Personal Equipment/ Response Protocols); and Private
Investigator pre-licensure and continuing education.
• Executive Board Member - National Council of Investigation and Security Services (NCISS), the
principal national trade association representing licensed private investigators and security
professionals before federal agencies and Congress.
• Legislative Chairman - South Carolina Association of Legal Investigators (SCALI).
• Monitoring Center Administrator - GPS and Home Incarceration Program monitoring center,
providing 24/7 location-tracking and compliance services to court systems and law-enforcement
agencies.
These qualifications inform every element of the enclosed comment. As a Chief Pilot for a county
Emergency Management Service, I have personal, on-scene experience with the time-sensitive nature of
SAR and hazmat operations and the operational impossibility of facility-by-facility notification during an
active incident. As a Level Ill thermographer and Part 107 commercial operator, I understand the
routine, lawful, low-altitude missions that the proposed rule risks foreclosing if implemented without
refinement. As a SLED-licensed investigator with 38 years of practice and a national-board obligation
through NCISS, I have a particular concern that the rule provide a clear credibility pathway for
accountable, vetted commercial operators rather than treating all unmanned-aircraft activity as
presumptively suspect. And as the administrator of a GPS monitoring center, I am keenly aware ofthe
cybersecurity, data-retention, and chain-of-custody implications of any new federally mandated
notification or tracking system - issues addressed directly in the enclosed comment.
The enclosed comment is offered constructively. It supports the FAA's implementation of section 2209
of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 and the framework of proposed part 74, while
identifying eight specific areas where targeted refinements would better balance the protection of
critical infrastructure against the statutory public right of transit under 49 U.S.C. § 40103 and the
legitimate operational needs of Public Safety and accountable commercial aviators.
I am available to expand on any point in the enclosed comment, to provide additional operational detail
from county Emergency Management or SAR experience, or to coordinate further input through NCISS
or SCALI as the rulemaking proceeds. Thank you for your consideration and for the FAA's continued
work on the safe integration of unmanned aircraft into the National Airspace System.

Dear Administrator a

Drone usage is entirely distracting while performing work in a rail yard. We are unaware of who is performing the duty of flying the drones when they’re out which takes away saftey during saftey sensitive operations like having hazardous materials in railcars. Transportation employees do not need any more distractions when working in a life threatening field, we need to focus on the task on hand not on who is flying a drone watching us while we are doing a duty that is dangerous enough as is.

Working for the railroad and knowing the risk we take daily moving various chemicals around. When I see that our armed forces are having an extremely difficult time fighting because of drones being used it triggers the thought of potential destruction in the US from drone attacks on our trains that we move through major cities of high populations.
Normally if we saw a drone we would report it. However, due to the fact that our management doesn’t want to physically come out into the elements that we bare everyday as railroads and observe us work, they use drones as a substitute for having to walk and participate as they should. Drones being controlled by management has caused us to become callous to seeing them near our yard and equipment and we no longer report them because it’s usually management so we suspect and a waste of time and effort to make that call while working. If drones we not allowed we would definitely report them and potentially stop the threat of an attack that could harm millions if someone were to attach an explosive to our hazardous cars. Please stop these drones from being anywhere near a train, train yard or train tracks so that we can get back to reporting them when we see them for our safety and the safety of our country and citizens in it. It is the most dangerous substitute for management to their daily duties as opposed to being present and keeping us all on alert. We carry explosives, corrosives, and flammable tank cars that can easily be taken advantage of by any foreign threat. Please get rid of them. Thank you

This is in refrence to flying drones anywhere near railyards. With 15 years at a railroad drones would just make things worse production and safety wise. We have enough distractions as is, we dont need more. Allowing drones overhead would just invite foul play, dangerous acts, and threats to lives and railroad cars. Let alone privacy if we need to water the rocks. Drones are prone to weather conditions,internet connection and many other uncontrollable problems. Let a drone hit me in the head, I would be out of work for weeks! All thanks to this ridiculous suggestion. What if its a drone thats not from our company and we come complacent with them being around only to find out there is a gun or other weapons attached
You further put our lives at risk all to hire one less person to get a job done , Shame! Shame!

I am a railroad employee with 22 years of experience in the field as a conductor and engineer.  When the railroads 1st advised that they were going to be using drones it sounded like they were going to use this new technology to improve safety by using them to scan tracks, trains, and property to identify defects, locate cars, and even trespassers on trains or railroad property to protect employees. In reality, 99% of the drone use that I know of has been used for the sole purpose of spying on their own employees.  Now every day I work I see conductors spending time looking to the skies worried about the next “drone strike” as we call it.  These actions are jeopardizing their own safety. We are taught during annual testing to recognize, record, and report strange activity on and around our workplace. I once reported a drone that was flying around me while I was switching a hazardous car loaded with propane.  The drone turned out to be railroad managers watching me but I had no idea of the drone’s intentions.  The managers subsequently approached me, and were very unhappy that I (1) stopped working and (2) alerted others about the drone (although I was only following rules that they put in place).  There is no way for me to differentiate a drone being flown by a railroad manager or a terrorist.
